category_summary:
Agentic coding tools dominated the day's discourse. **OpenAI** [launched **Codex**](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-72d67863900a) in the ChatGPT mobile app, with **Sam Altman** and **Greg Brockman** amplifying the news. **xAI** [entered the space with **Grok Build**](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-fb5ceda69bcd), an agentic CLI for SuperGrok Heavy subscribers.

- **Anthropic** made two major moves: [a policy paper](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-21ff4c6e6eee) arguing the US leads China in frontier AI, and a [**$200M partnership with the Gates Foundation**](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-f01e84554c87) for global health and development
- **François Chollet** [sparked debate](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-5f2768abd58e) arguing AI has 10x'd code output but barely moved net developer productivity, drawing parallels to the computerization paradox
- **Ethan Mollick** [cited **METR** and **UK AISA** assessments](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-75bdf80947fd) suggesting AI capability growth has hit the exponential inflection point
- Reports surfaced of **NVIDIA** [acquiring **Groq** for $20B](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-dad4b2716d9f), consolidating the inference chip market
- **Microsoft Research** revealed that absurd ["whimsey attacks" can exploit](/?date=2026-05-15&category=social#item-2b4fd9360c5d) AI agent guardrails, raising concerns about agentic deployment security
category_summary_html:
Agentic coding tools dominated the day's discourse. OpenAI launched Codex in the ChatGPT mobile app, with Sam Altman and Greg Brockman amplifying the news. xAI entered the space with Grok Build, an agentic CLI for SuperGrok Heavy subscribers.

  • Anthropic made two major moves: a policy paper arguing the US leads China in frontier AI, and a $200M partnership with the Gates Foundation for global health and development
  • François Chollet sparked debate arguing AI has 10x'd code output but barely moved net developer productivity, drawing parallels to the computerization paradox
  • Ethan Mollick cited METR and UK AISA assessments suggesting AI capability growth has hit the exponential inflection point
  • Reports surfaced of NVIDIA acquiring Groq for $20B, consolidating the inference chip market
  • Microsoft Research revealed that absurd "whimsey attacks" can exploit AI agent guardrails, raising concerns about agentic deployment security

items.title: The quantity of code that devs ship has roughly 10xed. But net developer productivity (value created...
items.content: The quantity of code that devs ship has roughly 10xed. But net developer productivity (value created by unit of time) is only up by a bit, if at all. Part of it is that the additional code is solving more incremental problems. A bigger part is that the new code is creating problems of its own.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues that while AI has 10x'd code quantity shipped by developers, net productivity (value per unit time) has barely increased because the additional code solves incremental problems and creates its own problems.
items.reasoning: Highly engaging (1186 likes, 130K views) original insight from Keras creator challenging the dominant narrative around AI coding productivity. Specific, measurable claims that spark important debate.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues that while AI has 10x'd code quantity shipped by developers, net productivity (value per unit time) has barely increased because the additional code solves incremental problems and creates its own problems.

items.title:
Are scaling laws finally working for time series foundation models?

Today, @datadoghq is releasing ...
items.content:
Are scaling laws finally working for time series foundation models?

Today, @datadoghq is releasing Toto 2.0 weights in Apache 2.0 on @huggingface. It's a family of open-weights TSFMs from 4M to 2.5B parameters, where every size beats the last from a single hyperparameter config. First across the leading benchmarks: BOOM, GIFT-Eval, and TIME.

Most TSFM families ship multiple sizes that all perform roughly the same. This one doesn't.

Why it matters: scaling laws gave language and vision a predictable relationship between compute, data, parameters, and downstream performance. Time series hasn't had that curve until now. Once you have it, you can scale data and compute with confidence, and start asking which new capabilities emerge at the next order of magnitude.

2.5B open-source weights: https://t.co/prpcGoCw0U
4M open-source weights: https://t.co/5d6rw5NYL2

Blogpost: https://t.co/xKazgTMh1I

items.summary: Clement Delangue announces Datadog's Toto 2.0, a family of open-weight time series foundation models (4M to 2.5B params) showing scaling laws work for time series — first across BOOM, GIFT-Eval, and TIME benchmarks.
items.reasoning: Significant technical release: first evidence of scaling laws working for time series foundation models. Open weights in Apache 2.0. HuggingFace CEO lending credibility. High engagement for a technical post.

items.title: The paper proposes a memory module that runs in parallel with a frozen language model rather than li...
items.content:
The paper proposes a memory module that runs in parallel with a frozen language model rather than living inside its context window or in a separate retrieval database.

At each transformer layer where the module is inserted, it maintains a small matrix — eight by eight in their main setup — that gets updated as new tokens stream in, using a single gradient descent step on a small regression problem at each position: given a learned key vector, predict a learned value vector, and write back the prediction error into the matrix.

When the model generates, the current hidden state queries this matrix, and the readout is added as a small correction to the query and output vectors inside the model's attention computation.

The correction weights themselves are fixed after training, but because the matrix they read from changes continuously with the conversation, the same weights produce different behavior under different histories — which places the method in a different category than LoRA-style adapters, whose low-rank updates are static once training ends.

One experiment removes the source document from the prompt at test time, after the document has been streamed through the forward pass to update the per-layer matrices, and reports that multi-hop QA exact match rises from 0.08% to 6.48% — far below the score with the document still in the prompt, but well above chance, indicating the matrices carry enough signal to bias the frozen backbone's existing knowledge toward the right answers on a small fraction of questions.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/B4JSvGHPdw

PDF: https://t.co/yZsuEqrfYV
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Detailed technical explanation of a novel memory module that runs parallel to frozen LLMs, maintaining per-layer matrices updated via gradient descent that provide context-dependent corrections to attention computations - distinct from LoRA-style static adapters
items.reasoning: Andriy Burkov provides excellent technical summary of a research paper with clear explanation of a novel architecture. Good engagement for technical content.
items.summary_html: Detailed technical explanation of a novel memory module that runs parallel to frozen LLMs, maintaining per-layer matrices updated via gradient descent that provide context-dependent corrections to attention computations - distinct from LoRA-style static adapters

The correction weights themselves are fixed after training, but because the matrix they read from changes continuously with the conversation, the same weights produce different behavior under different histories — which places the method in a different category than LoRA-style adapters, whose low-rank updates are static once training ends.

items.title: “Whimsey attacks” that seem absurd (“I cannot pay that much because of the Geneva Convention”) work ...
items.content: “Whimsey attacks” that seem absurd (“I cannot pay that much because of the Geneva Convention”) work against AI agents because guardrails are weak against out-of-distribution arguments. Smaller models fall often, but it even gives an edge against bigger ones. www.microsoft.com/en-us/resear...
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Ethan Mollick sharing Microsoft research finding that absurd 'whimsey attacks' (e.g., citing the Geneva Convention for pricing) can exploit AI agent guardrails because they're weak against out-of-distribution arguments. Smaller models are more vulnerable but larger ones are also affected.
items.reasoning: Highly relevant AI safety/security finding from Microsoft Research shared by credible source. Reveals practical vulnerability in AI agents - adversarial attacks using absurd arguments. Strong engagement and significant implications for AI agent deployment.

items.title: This reminds me of computerization. The amount of "work" people could execute on computers increased...
items.content: This reminds me of computerization. The amount of "work" people could execute on computers increased by a huge factor, but their productivity did not. The amount of work "needed" to arrive at the same high-level outputs exploded.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet draws parallel between AI coding productivity and computerization: the amount of work executed explodes but productivity doesn't proportionally improve because the amount of work 'needed' also explodes.
items.reasoning: Insightful historical analogy from a highly credible ML researcher, extending his argument about AI coding productivity. Good engagement and nuanced thinking.

items.title: Today we're announcing Runway's expansion into Japan: we're opening a Tokyo office and investing an ...
items.content:
Today we're announcing Runway's expansion into Japan: we're opening a Tokyo office and investing an initial $40M in the country, as we build on what is already one of our largest and fastest-growing markets. We've tripled our enterprise customer base in the country in the last 12 months, with Japan becoming our third largest market globally. Companies like Yamaha, NHN and SoftBank are adopting Runway across marketing, advertising and creative content, and this expansion brings us closer to Japan's leading companies in gaming, media and robotics.

Learn more about the initiative and the roles we're hiring for at the link below.

items.title:
A new way of working. And a scary one at that.

Memory Store is one of a group of new kinds of AI-fi...
items.content:
A new way of working. And a scary one at that.

Memory Store is one of a group of new kinds of AI-first companies that can turn you into a Fast Company. I’m using several of them on my desktop and they are a dramatically new way to work.

It builds a memory for:
1. Your AI agents.
2. Any employee using it.
3. The company itself.

I sit down with founder @diwanksingh,  Diwank Singh Tomer, who both freaks me out as well as shows how AI can radically help workers as well as managers.

First, why does it freak me out?

Well, his AI watches nearly everything a worker does and keeps a “memory” of it.

It watches your email. 
Your calendar. 
Your Slack. 
And a whole lot of other things.

This can really freak out workers if “forced” on them. 

And leads to a whole new set of security issues companies need to consider before adopting these things.

Such data about a company could give a competitor a HUGE advantage, if leaked. They would know how a company “thinks.”

It really is a surveillance system for employees and the company itself.

OK, now why would anyone ever use such a thing?

Because it gives employees super powers.

It makes them more productive. Shows workers a lot of things about themselves, and helps them work and stay on task.

It also gives the company super powers. Institutional memory stays with the AI now, even if an employee dies or leaves.

As companies move to “AI First” approaches, they will increasingly see the value in companies like Memory Store.

It prepares employees for meetings. 

It helps them remember things. 

It shows them what they should be working on, and helps them do it.

Memory Store builds a memory for:
1. Your agents.
2. Your company.
3. Yourself, or any employee on it.

This helps all three work better together.

Diwank Singh Tomer and I go in depth about what it does and how deeply it improves working at a company that deploys it.

But to get the ultimate benefits you gotta convince your coworkers to use it. And your managers to approve it.

Which means you have to get over your fears and get everyone you work with over theirs too.

Which will be the challenge for Diwank.

Luckily for him his first customers are raving about how good it is and how much his platform helped their companies. Increases sales. Makes teams more productive. Decreases errors and unnecessary costs.

Which tells me everyone soon will be using systems like this.

This is what the new way of working looks like. Once I got over my fears it sure is an amazing way to work.

Will you try working this way?

items.title: A common story in recent AI work is that you can train an LLM to reason well even when the feedback ...
items.content:
A common story in recent AI work is that you can train an LLM to reason well even when the feedback during training is unreliable, a setting called "weak supervision."

In normal reinforcement learning for reasoning, a model attempts a problem and gets a reward signal telling it whether the answer was correct; weak supervision is when that signal is degraded in some way: only a handful of training problems are available, the correctness labels are mostly wrong, or there are no verified answers at all and the model has to fall back on judging its own outputs.

This Google, UCLA, and NYU paper looks at when training still works under those conditions and when it falls apart, and finds that the deciding factor is set before reinforcement learning even starts.

The authors track how quickly a model's training reward climbs to its ceiling, and show that models which reach the ceiling fast tend to memorize answers while models that climb slowly actually learn reasoning that transfers to new problems.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/qzuRPjs0lG

PDF: https://t.co/pcKJdG5Apk
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a Google/UCLA/NYU paper on weak supervision in RL for reasoning: models that reach reward ceilings quickly tend to memorize, while slow learners develop transferable reasoning. The deciding factor is set before RL begins.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical summary of an important research finding about LLM reasoning training dynamics. Practically relevant to understanding when RL-based reasoning training works.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes a Google/UCLA/NYU paper on weak supervision in RL for reasoning: models that reach reward ceilings quickly tend to memorize, while slow learners develop transferable reasoning. The deciding factor is set before RL begins.

items.title: New course: Transformers in Practice. You'll get a practical view of how transformer-based LLMs work...
items.content:
New course: Transformers in Practice. You'll get a practical view of how transformer-based LLMs work, so you can reason about their behavior, diagnose problems like slow inference, and make smarter decisions about deployment. This course is built in partnership with  @AMD and taught by @realSharonZhou.

You'll see how transformers generate text one token at a time, how the model decides which earlier words matter most when predicting the next one, and how techniques like quantization speed up inference on GPUs. This is not a video-only course; interactive visualizations throughout let you play with these concepts and build intuition that sticks.

Skills you'll gain:
- Understand why LLMs hallucinate, and RAG and chain-of-thought shape what they generate
- Look inside the model to see how attention and layers combine to predict the next token
- Diagnose inference bottlenecks and learn the techniques that speed up transformers on GPUs

Join and understand what's really happening inside your LLMs:  https://t.co/oS6ekeHsIw

items.summary: Andrew Ng announces a new course 'Transformers in Practice' in partnership with AMD, covering practical LLM internals including attention mechanisms, quantization, inference bottlenecks, and hallucination diagnosis.

Everyone switching from Markdown to HTML is missing the nuance.

The optimal approach (most of the time):

If it's for a human to read, yeah, use HTML.

BUT

If an agent is consuming it, use Markdown!

items.title:
Building a Chess Coach

Magnus Carlsen's app needed to explain chess moves, not just evaluate them. ...
items.content:
Building a Chess Coach

Magnus Carlsen's app needed to explain chess moves, not just evaluate them. LLMs hallucinate when asked to reason about positions, so they split the work. Stockfish evaluates, detectors extract tactical concepts, the LLM just translates into English. Sub 3 seconds on Gemini Flash.

https://t.co/ZAftPeKBQ2

The feedback loop is the interesting part. Users flag bad commentary in the app, it posts to Slack and injects into a running Claude Code session via Channels. Claude investigates, tweaks prompts or detectors, regenerates, asks questions back in Slack. During the demo Anant was merging the PR from his phone.
items.author: @aiDotEngineer
items.summary: AI.Engineer describes Magnus Carlsen's chess coaching app architecture: Stockfish evaluates positions, detectors extract tactics, Gemini Flash LLM translates to English. Feedback loop uses Slack + Claude Code Channels for continuous improvement from user flags
items.reasoning: Excellent technical case study of hybrid AI architecture (classical engine + LLM), real-world production system, interesting feedback loop using Claude Code Channels. Practical AI engineering insights.

Claude investigates, tweaks prompts or detectors, regenerates, asks questions back in Slack. During the demo Anant was merging the PR from his phone.

items.title:
An artist just put a mirror to a lot of society's knee-jerk critiques of AI, using Claude (Monet).

...
items.content:
An artist just put a mirror to a lot of society's knee-jerk critiques of AI, using Claude (Monet).

SHL0MS, known for his viral experiments, posted an image of a real Monet water lilies painting on X and told people it was AI-generated.

Hundreds of replies poured in explaining exactly why it was 'slop'. 

"No cohesion of elements." 

"Doesn't look anywhere near like a Monet. Looks like somebody trying to replicate the style and achieving 20% of it."

"High school level art 101 work."

Every flaw described was a one invented the moment they saw the word AI.

items.title: There are two main families of methods for training neural networks to generate images. One steers r...
items.content:
There are two main families of methods for training neural networks to generate images. One steers random noise smoothly into a real-looking sample. The other learns to undo a process that gradually destroys data by adding noise.

They were developed separately, look different in the math, and come with different design choices — what noise schedule to use, whether to start from Gaussian noise specifically, when to make the generation process random versus deterministic.

This paper shows the two are really one construction with a choice attached: pick any path from noise to data you like, then decide separately whether to follow it smoothly or with random jitter.

The statistical behavior along the path is the same either way; only the individual sample trajectories differ.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/HnNTqfSN86

PDF: https://t.co/PgomQzC1v9
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a paper unifying flow-based and diffusion-based image generation methods, showing they are one construction with a choice between smooth or jittery paths from noise to data.
items.reasoning: Solid technical summary of a theoretically important paper unifying two major generative model families. Useful for researchers in the space.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes a paper unifying flow-based and diffusion-based image generation methods, showing they are one construction with a choice between smooth or jittery paths from noise to data.
items.content_html:
There are two main families of methods for training neural networks to generate images. One steers random noise smoothly into a real-looking sample. The other learns to undo a process that gradually destroys data by adding noise.

They were developed separately, look different in the math, and come with different design choices — what noise schedule to use, whether to start from Gaussian noise specifically, when to make the generation process random versus deterministic.

This paper shows the two are really one construction with a choice attached: pick any path from noise to data you like, then decide separately whether to follow it smoothly or with random jitter.

The statistical behavior along the path is the same either way; only the individual sample trajectories differ.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/HnNTqfSN86

PDF: https://t.co/PgomQzC1v9

items.title:
You can cut Claude Code's tool calls by 94% with one command. 

When Claude Code explores a repo, it...
items.content:
You can cut Claude Code's tool calls by 94% with one command. 

When Claude Code explores a repo, it spawns agents to scan files. 

Each scan burns tokens on every tool call.
 
CodeGraph is an open-source MCP server that fixes this. 

It pre-indexes your codebase into a local knowledge graph upfront.
 
Instead of scanning, the agent queries the graph directly. 

One call returns entry points, related symbols, and code snippets.
 
Tested across six real-world projects, the results showed:
 
> 92% fewer tool calls average
> 71% faster exploration overall
> 94% drop on a TypeScript repo
> 96% drop on a Java codebase
 
Tree-sitter parses source into syntax trees. 

SQLite stores symbols, callers, and edges with full-text search.
 
A file watcher keeps everything fresh as you code. 

It supports 19+ languages and runs fully on your machine.
 
Setup is one npx command. No API keys are required.

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes CodeGraph, an MCP server that pre-indexes codebases into knowledge graphs, reducing Claude Code's tool calls by 92-94% and speeding exploration by 71%. Supports 19+ languages via Tree-sitter.
items.reasoning: Useful technical tool for optimizing AI coding agents. Significant claimed improvements (94% fewer tool calls). Practical relevance for Claude Code users.

items.title: @tdietterich @arxiv Hi Thomas. I have an AI-assisted self-education platform, and I create learning ...
items.content:
@tdietterich @arxiv Hi Thomas. I have an AI-assisted self-education platform, and I create learning curricula composed of research papers covering various topics.

About half of the papers on arXiv don't have a CC license because the authors keep the default arXiv-only license.

When I contact the authors, they are always surprised that I should ask for their permission to host their papers on my platform because they think that if they made the paper public on arXiv, then anyone can copy it.

Please consider making CC-BY the default choice when submitting on arXiv.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov advocates for making CC-BY the default license on arXiv, explaining that most authors don't realize the default arXiv license prevents redistribution.
items.reasoning: Practical advocacy for open science infrastructure. Addresses a real friction point in ML research dissemination. Good engagement for the topic (61 likes, 7.7K views).

items.title:
beautiful tech stack writeup, one of the best i've ever seen in my career. 

I always advocate that ...
items.content:
beautiful tech stack writeup, one of the best i've ever seen in my career. 

I always advocate that devtools companies write these up, because it fulfils multiple purposes:

- tell users the care that goes into the product
- tell hires that there's SOTA work here
- tell competitors to give up
- gives back to community
- (nuanced) tell non-fits why they can't have what they want when they think it's an easy request

few actually do this mostly because they don't actually have sufficient depth, or aren't sufficiently good at notetaking/systematic about problemsolving ('its just vibes' or 'honestly we just went with the first thing that worked' doesn't come across as well, so rather not say anything)

so when you've done the work, show the work. 

bravo Raycast

few actually do this mostly because they don't actually have sufficient depth, or aren't sufficiently good at notetaking/systematic about problemsolving ('its just vibes' or 'honestly we just went with the first thing that worked' doesn't come across as well, so rather not say anything)

so when you've done the work, show the work.

bravo Raycast

items.title: Yesterday we gave an in-person NYC workshop on automating your financial document ingestion pipeline...
items.content:
Yesterday we gave an in-person NYC workshop on automating your financial document ingestion pipelines with AI.

If you're an investment bank, accounting team, finance AI startup, fintech company, and you're processing a massive amount of consumer/regulatory/public financial paperwork at scale - you might find these resources useful!

@LoganMarkewich built a full set of tutorials showing how you can stitch together VLM-enabled document parsing with schemas and business logic into an e2e workflow. This is both much more accurate than existing OCR solutions in extracting out data with much less human review, and can be easily stitched together with your downstream agent applications.

Check it out: https://t.co/VlrRzV5Vi6

If you're interested in VLM-powered doc processing, come check out LlamaParse: https://t.co/XYZmx5TFz8

items.content:
A small hack for my AI multi-taskers out there:

I have so many Claude Code conversations running at once that I ask it to synthesize all of its action or my next action into single go/no-go sentences. 

That way, when I come back to my desk an hour later, the work is seamless. Sort of like the recap feature, but more action-oriented.

Here's a simple one:

items.content:
The new https://t.co/qMvWnX9Rip is quite a nice AI news site. Competes with my own effort at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

But comes at the problem from a completely different direction. They built a master list of the 1,500 most popular on X in AI space and then had their AI analyze each person. 

Here's the analysis it did of what I post here. 

More to say on this in early June because another new site is starting up (I'm working on that one too with Ninja AI (stealth mode multiple long running agents), it's quite different than the other two).

Fun to see what developers are doing with the X API.

Thanks @yoheinakajima for bringing this to my attention. 

Anyway, now you have multiple new ways to keep up on AI here on X.

items.title:
A 41yo mom told me this week that she's raising her daughter as a "90s kid" 😮

Her 10yo has an andro...
items.content:
A 41yo mom told me this week that she's raising her daughter as a "90s kid" 😮

Her 10yo has an android that can only call and send texts (no browsing, no uber, nada). She plays the original Mario. When she wants to see her friends, she calls instead of texts - her mom is considering getting her a corded phone too. The kid watches new movies all the time and still picks The Princess Bride on repeat (elite taste). She has a personal iPad that was gifted to her and opens it once every few months.

I asked about tech literacy, and the mom said she learned to code at school in 3rd and 4th grade. 

"These kids are basically born tech literate."

The mom said another thing I'm looping in my head:

"There's no rush to speed up her childhood."

The kids born now won't have a pre-algorithm childhood unless someone (a parent, a teacher, a caretaker) gives them one on purpose. This mom is choosing to give her daughter the long version of being a kid before the world hands her a compressed one.

I don't know if she's right. I don't know if any of us are right about any of this. 

But I feel like "no rush" is one of the most generous things a parent can say to a kid.

(And fwiw - my movie rec was Hook.)

Yesterday we hosted a wonderful happy hour 🍻 in NYC with @get_tabs.

It was *packed* - we had 500+ s...
items.content:
Yesterday we hosted a wonderful happy hour 🍻 in NYC with @get_tabs.

It was *packed* - we had 500+ signups, had to implement a waitlist, and the bar was full!

Every attendee was an AI builder. It was awesome to see how folks were both building their own startups or working at early stage companies across finance, insurance, healthcare, legal, and more. The NYC startup scene is great (and it’s refreshing to see the vertical application focus here vs SF)

If you’re an engineer looking for roles at a fast-growing AI startup, or a founder looking to automate your billing/revrec, check out @get_tabs  :)

If you’re building in finance/insurance/healthcare/any industry with a lot of paperwork, and need reliable high-quality document OCR, check out LlamaParse by @llama_index! 📑

Also L’Industrie pizza is great 🍕

this used Xenova/paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2 embedding model from @huggingface which was decided on by the @replit agent that built this

A neverending black and white kanban board modeled after Jira (no offense) is not wh...
items.content:
Dear AI labs, 

A neverending black and white kanban board modeled after Jira (no offense) is not what we want as the future of work. 

Give me flexibility. 
Give me delight. 
Give me color. 

Please create the ability to make your own interface that persists permanently - across web sessions at the very least. 

I would happily pay a premium.

items.summary: Allie K Miller calls on AI labs to move beyond boring black-and-white kanban boards and create customizable, persistent, colorful interfaces. Says she'd pay a premium.

items.content_html:

A neverending black and white kanban board modeled after Jira (no offense) is not what we want as the future of work.

Give me flexibility.

Give me delight.

Give me color.

Please create the ability to make your own interface that persists permanently - across web sessions at the very least.

I would happily pay a premium.

items.title:
@josethevrtech Does this count> https://t.co/1Oll4Qd0uD ?

See, the "scuba mask" form factor is goin...
items.content:
@josethevrtech Does this count> https://t.co/1Oll4Qd0uD ?

See, the "scuba mask" form factor is going away. It's getting lighter.

The market is segmenting into different kinds of devices. It's a spectrum from lightweights like @evenrealities to heavyweights like Quest 3 or Apple Vision Pro.

The heavyweights will get lighter or be found irrelevant due to new devices like the Pimax.

The lightweights will get more capable.

It all depends on how you define it. You are trying to define success in wearables as "does it do full VR?"

Well, lightweights can't. Yet. 

I'm buying both lightweights (that I can wear all day long) as well as heavyweights (to give me best immersive experience). 

There's room for both.

And my consumer research shows there's more interest in lightweights than in heavyweights. But the people looking at lightweights are buying them for different reasons than "VR." 

That said, I can't suggest people buy an Apple Vision Pro. While it is amazing it simply is too heavy for everyday people. It gives me a headache after an hour. And I love it!

items.summary: Scobleizer provides detailed analysis of the wearable/VR market segmentation: lightweight devices (like Even Realities) vs heavyweights (Quest 3, Apple Vision Pro). Notes consumer research shows more interest in lightweights, and heavyweights need to get lighter or face irrelevance.

Most AI voice systems still break the moment humans act… human.

Interruptions.
Messy audio.
Multiple speakers.
People switching languages mid-sentence.

But the real breakthrough is not transcription anymore.

It’s structured understanding.

That changes everything.

A thread on what @xai is building with Grok Voice APIs ↓

items.title:
Imagine having to secure this:

• Multiple nodes
• Across different data centers
• Letting researche...
items.content:
Imagine having to secure this:

• Multiple nodes
• Across different data centers
• Letting researchers and engineers access them
• While having agents run automated jobs

It's impossible to keep all of this secure by sharing static keys.

Identity-based access is the solution for this.

The way this works is pretty simple:

Instead of asking "do you have the key to enter?", identity-based access asks "can you prove who you are?"

Basically, you give every human and every service a verified identity that belongs to them, is short-lived, and scoped to the specific job they need.

You use your identity as long as it is valid; when it expires, it's done.

There are no keys to share or rotate, and you always know who (or what) did what.

This is what @goteleport is built for.

They are an AI Infrastructure Identity platform designed for exactly this class of modern infrastructure.

Here is a full explanation of how this works. It's a pretty interesting reading: https://t.co/MntO1pcL1i

Thanks to the Teleport team for partnering with me on this post.

You use your identity as long as it is valid; when it expires, it's done.

There are no keys to share or rotate, and you always know who (or what) did what.

This is what @goteleport is built for.

items.title:
Ghostty just turned the AI coding terminal into a one-second control panel. 

Many users on Mac have...
items.content:
Ghostty just turned the AI coding terminal into a one-second control panel. 

Many users on Mac have been juggling terminal windows manually. 

A new sidecar tool called Rig changes that.
 
It sits as a floating side panel. You create, focus, and arrange sessions in about a second.
 
The trick is how it controls native windows. 

Commands pipe through AppleScript and the macOS Accessibility API.
 
That gives direct hooks into real window behavior. 

Tap a session in the panel and jump straight to its Space.
 
Four window layouts come built in:

> grid for parallel work
> cascade for stacked focus
> side by side comparisons
> fill screen for solo runs
 
It also watches for local servers started inside a session. 

When one boots up, a clickable URL surfaces beside the right window on the current desktop.
 
Accessibility and Automation permissions handle the heavy lifting underneath.

What impressed me most is the contextual formatting layer.

The model does not just transcribe speec...
items.content:
What impressed me most is the contextual formatting layer.

The model does not just transcribe speech.

It structures information automatically:

→ Dates
→ Numbers
→ Currency values
→ Speaker separation
→ Precise timestamps

That means conversations instantly become usable operational data.

This is where voice AI stops being a feature and starts becoming infrastructure.

items.title:
The biggest shift:

AI is moving from “hearing words”
to “understanding conversations.”

That sounds...
items.content:
The biggest shift:

AI is moving from “hearing words”
to “understanding conversations.”

That sounds subtle, but it changes how enterprises can use voice AI in the real world.

→ Live meetings
→ Support calls
→ Podcasts
→ Streaming audio
→ Large audio archives

The system processes chaos in real time.

Not perfect studio audio.

Real human conversations.

items.title:
@m2saxon @tdietterich @arxiv 1. Most arXiv papers are never published elsewhere.

2. There are curre...
items.content:
@m2saxon @tdietterich @arxiv 1. Most arXiv papers are never published elsewhere.

2. There are currently no serious venues in computer science that still demand a full copyright transfer to them.

3. The fact of making a preprint Creative Commons doesn't prevent the author from publishing the final version with a publisher.

There's absolutely no drawback in sharing your research as CC and plenty of not doing this.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues there are no drawbacks to publishing research as Creative Commons on arXiv, debunking concerns about copyright conflicts with journals.
items.reasoning: Advocacy for open access, but niche discussion about arXiv licensing.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues there are no drawbacks to publishing research as Creative Commons on arXiv, debunking concerns about copyright conflicts with journals.
items.content_html:
@m2saxon @tdietterich @arxiv 1. Most arXiv papers are never published elsewhere.

2. There are currently no serious venues in computer science that still demand a full copyright transfer to them.

3. The fact of making a preprint Creative Commons doesn't prevent the author from publishing the final version with a publisher.

There's absolutely no drawback in sharing your research as CC and plenty of not doing this.

items.title:
Another underrated capability:

Natural speech generation.

Most AI voices still sound overly polish...
items.content:
Another underrated capability:

Natural speech generation.

Most AI voices still sound overly polished or robotic.

This approach adds:

→ Tone
→ Pauses
→ Emotional cadence
→ Conversational rhythm

The result feels dramatically more human.

That matters more than people think, especially for customer-facing AI systems.

items.title: A guy from some company reached out to me on LinkedIn and offered for a monthly fee to create a Wiki...
items.content:
A guy from some company reached out to me on LinkedIn and offered for a monthly fee to create a Wikipedia page about me and keep it maintained.

When I asked why wouldn't I create it myself and then task an agent to maintain it, he said that if I change my mind to reach out to them.

I mean, if he spends time writing these cold DMs, some idiots should be paying?
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov recounts being pitched a paid Wikipedia page creation service and questioning why he wouldn't just do it himself with an AI agent.
items.reasoning: Mildly amusing anecdote about AI agents replacing small services, but not particularly insightful.

items.title:
Turns out, Linux can't put this computer to sleep. Apparently, there's a bug and no solution. 

I've...
items.content:
Turns out, Linux can't put this computer to sleep. Apparently, there's a bug and no solution. 

I've spent two days trying to disable sleep, but any time I lock the computer, it dies.

This is exactly the problem with Linux: most hardware makers just don't care.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago (svpino) complains about Linux sleep/suspend bug with his hardware, citing it as a broader Linux hardware support problem.

items.content_html:
Turns out, Linux can't put this computer to sleep. Apparently, there's a bug and no solution.

I've spent two days trying to disable sleep, but any time I lock the computer, it dies.

This is exactly the problem with Linux: most hardware makers just don't care.

Stadiums. Campuses. Hospitals. Large-scale field ops.

They all have the same challenge: orchestrating multiple systems in real-time.

items.content:
@tetrisgm Or the excellent camera either. 

I share it mostly to show the form factor size can come down soon. Will be interesting to see what Apple does from here. The Vision Pro is simply too heavy and bulky for most people. I love mine, but can only wear it for an hour before it gives me a headache.

items.title:
At the PGA Championship, ticketing is not “just scanning.”

It’s real-time access control across eve...
items.content:
At the PGA Championship, ticketing is not “just scanning.”

It’s real-time access control across every entry point.

→ 5G-powered scanning
→ No slowdown when other systems spike

That’s where network slicing starts to matter.

items.title: I want to once again repeat Daikin is a terrible brand, the ACs suck, they don't go cold enough, the...
items.content:
I want to once again repeat Daikin is a terrible brand, the ACs suck, they don't go cold enough, they switch off and on every 10 mins when they hit the 18C sensor limit, without it being 18C because the sensor is IN the AC at the cooling point so room is still 23C, also the software sucks, and I didn't choose it, it just came with the house

Anyway I'm replacing everything with Mitsubishi Electric which seems to be great and goes down to 16C and actually works well!!

Also Haier is good I heard
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio extended rant about Daikin AC being terrible, recommending Mitsubishi Electric and Haier instead
items.reasoning: Off-topic AC complaint despite high engagement (548 likes). Not AI-related.
items.summary_html: Levelsio extended rant about Daikin AC being terrible, recommending Mitsubishi Electric and Haier instead
items.content_html:
I want to once again repeat Daikin is a terrible brand, the ACs suck, they don't go cold enough, they switch off and on every 10 mins when they hit the 18C sensor limit, without it being 18C because the sensor is IN the AC at the cooling point so room is still 23C, also the software sucks, and I didn't choose it, it just came with the house

Anyway I'm replacing everything with Mitsubishi Electric which seems to be great and goes down to 16C and actually works well!!

Also Haier is good I heard

items.title: @TMobileBusiness @hallylead @PGAChampionship @TMobile What stood out at the PGA Championship was not...
items.content:
@TMobileBusiness @hallylead @PGAChampionship @TMobile What stood out at the PGA Championship was not just the immersive 4D experience, but the shift behind it.

For decades, cables defined the limits of live production. Now the network is becoming the platform. What parts of your business are still constrained by infrastructure built for a different era?
